Real Analysis

Completeness, Suprema of Transformed Sets, and the Archimedean Property

Completeness, Suprema of Transformed Sets, and the Archimedean Property

Completeness Property

Lecture 3 introduced [[Supremum|sup⁡S\sup SsupS]] and [[Infimum|inf⁡S\inf SinfS]] via a two-part definition — upper bound, plus least upper bound. In practice, the second part is the hard one to verify directly, since it quantifies over every possible upper bound. We start by tightening this into a checkable criterion before putting suprema to work.

Supremum (Least Upper Bound)

Let S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty. A number u∈Ru \in \mathbb{R}u∈R is the supremum of SSS, written sup⁡S=u\sup S = usupS=u, if:

  • uuu is an upper bound of SSS: for all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, s≤us \le us≤u, and
  • for every upper bound vvv of SSS, u≤vu \le vu≤v.

To confirm a candidate uuu actually equals sup⁡S\sup SsupS, two things must be checked: that uuu is an upper bound, and that it is the least one. A common way to verify the second condition is to instead show that no smaller number can be an upper bound — i.e., every v<uv < uv<u fails to bound SSS from above. This reformulation is captured precisely below.

Supremum Characterizations

Let S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty and u∈Ru \in \mathbb{R}u∈R. The following are equivalent:

  1. u=sup⁡Su = \sup Su=supS.
  2. uuu is an upper bound of SSS, and for all v<uv < uv<u, there exists s∈Ss \in Ss∈S with v<s≤uv < s \le uv<s≤u.
  3. uuu is an upper bound of SSS, and for all ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0, there exists s∈Ss \in Ss∈S with u−ε<s≤uu - \varepsilon < s \le uu−ε<s≤u.
Relating (2) and (3)

Condition (3) is just condition (2) with the substitution v=u−εv = u - \varepsilonv=u−ε for ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0. Phrasing it in terms of ε\varepsilonε is often more convenient in practice, since it makes “how close” sss must come to uuu explicit.

Shifted Set Supremum

With a workable characterization of sup⁡S\sup SsupS in hand, we can now ask how the supremum behaves under natural operations on SSS itself — starting with translation.

Translation of a Set

Let a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R and S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty. Define a+S={a+s:s∈S}a + S = \{a + s : s \in S\}a+S={a+s:s∈S}.

Supremum of a Translated Set

For any a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R and nonempty S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R, sup⁡(a+S)=a+sup⁡S.\sup(a+S) = a + \sup S.sup(a+S)=a+supS.

Proof

Let u=sup⁡Su = \sup Su=supS; we show a+u=sup⁡(a+S)a + u = \sup(a+S)a+u=sup(a+S) by checking both parts of the definition.

a+ua+ua+u is an upper bound of a+Sa+Sa+S. For any s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, s≤us \le us≤u. Adding aaa preserves the inequality: a+s≤a+ua + s \le a + ua+s≤a+u. Hence a+ua + ua+u bounds a+Sa + Sa+S from above.

a+ua+ua+u is the least upper bound. Let vvv be any upper bound of a+Sa+Sa+S. Then a+s≤va+s \le va+s≤v for all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, so s≤v−as \le v - as≤v−a. Thus v−av - av−a is an upper bound of SSS, giving u≤v−au \le v - au≤v−a, i.e. a+u≤va + u \le va+u≤v.

Since a+ua+ua+u is an upper bound and no smaller upper bound exists, sup⁡(a+S)=a+sup⁡S\sup(a+S) = a + \sup Ssup(a+S)=a+supS.

Scaled Set Supremum

Scaling by a constant is the natural next transformation to consider, but — as with the order properties in Lecture 3 — the sign of the constant matters: scaling by a negative number swaps the roles of supremum and infimum.

Scaling of a Set

Let a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R and S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R be nonempty. Define aS={as:s∈S}aS = \{as : s \in S\}aS={as:s∈S}.

Supremum of a Scaled Set

For nonempty S⊆RS \subseteq \mathbb{R}S⊆R and a∈Ra \in \mathbb{R}a∈R,

sup⁡(aS)={a⋅sup⁡S,if a>0,a⋅inf⁡S,if a<0,0,if a=0 (since aS={0}).\sup(aS) = \begin{cases} a \cdot \sup S, & \text{if } a > 0,\\ a \cdot \inf S, & \text{if } a < 0,\\ 0, & \text{if } a = 0 \ (\text{since } aS = \{0\}). \end{cases}sup(aS)=⎩⎨⎧​a⋅supS,a⋅infS,0,​if a>0,if a<0,if a=0 (since aS={0}).​
Proof

Case a>0a > 0a>0. For all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, s≤sup⁡Ss \le \sup Ss≤supS implies as≤asup⁡Sas \le a\sup Sas≤asupS (order is preserved when multiplying by a positive, as in Lecture 3), so asup⁡Sa\sup SasupS is an upper bound of aSaSaS. If vvv is any upper bound of aSaSaS, then as≤vas \le vas≤v for all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S; dividing by a>0a > 0a>0 gives s≤v/as \le v/as≤v/a, so v/av/av/a is an upper bound of SSS. Hence sup⁡S≤v/a\sup S \le v/asupS≤v/a, i.e. asup⁡S≤va\sup S \le vasupS≤v. Thus sup⁡(aS)=asup⁡S\sup(aS) = a \sup Ssup(aS)=asupS.

Case a<0a < 0a<0. For all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S, inf⁡S≤s\inf S \le sinfS≤s implies as≤ainf⁡Sas \le a \inf Sas≤ainfS — the inequality reverses because aaa is negative — so ainf⁡Sa \inf SainfS is an upper bound of aSaSaS. If vvv is an upper bound of aSaSaS, then as≤vas \le vas≤v for all s∈Ss \in Ss∈S; dividing by a<0a < 0a<0 reverses the inequality to s≥v/as \ge v/as≥v/a, so v/av/av/a is a lower bound of SSS. Hence v/a≤inf⁡Sv/a \le \inf Sv/a≤infS, i.e. v≥ainf⁡Sv \ge a \inf Sv≥ainfS. Thus sup⁡(aS)=ainf⁡S\sup(aS) = a \inf Ssup(aS)=ainfS.

Case a=0a = 0a=0. Then aS={0}aS = \{0\}aS={0}, so sup⁡(aS)=0\sup(aS) = 0sup(aS)=0.

Why inf⁡S\inf SinfS appears when a<0a<0a<0

This is the key subtlety of the theorem: multiplying by a negative number flips the direction of every inequality, which turns “upper bound” into “lower bound” and vice versa. The supremum of aSaSaS is therefore governed by the infimum of SSS, not its supremum — a direct echo of the sign-flip behavior proved for order in Lecture 3.

Order Relation Between Two Sets

Having seen how sup⁡\supsup and inf⁡\infinf interact with operations on a single set, we now compare suprema and infima across two sets, whenever every element of one dominates every element of the other.

Comparing sup⁡A\sup AsupA and inf⁡B\inf BinfB

If A,B⊆RA, B \subseteq \mathbb{R}A,B⊆R are nonempty and a≤ba \le ba≤b for all a∈Aa \in Aa∈A and b∈Bb \in Bb∈B, then sup⁡A≤inf⁡B.\sup A \le \inf B.supA≤infB.

Proof

Since a≤ba \le ba≤b for all a∈Aa \in Aa∈A, b∈Bb \in Bb∈B, each fixed a∈Aa \in Aa∈A is a lower bound of BBB. Hence a≤inf⁡Ba \le \inf Ba≤infB for all a∈Aa \in Aa∈A, which makes inf⁡B\inf BinfB an upper bound of AAA. Therefore sup⁡A≤inf⁡B\sup A \le \inf BsupA≤infB.

Completeness Property of ℝ

The three results above all assume suprema and infima exist and study how they behave. Their existence in the first place is not a consequence of the field and order axioms — it is an independent axiom of R\mathbb{R}R, restated here from Lecture 3 for reference, since everything in the remainder of this lecture rests on it.

Completeness (Least Upper Bound) Property

Every nonempty subset A⊆RA \subseteq \mathbb{R}A⊆R that is bounded above has a supremum in R\mathbb{R}R, and every nonempty subset that is bounded below has an infimum in R\mathbb{R}R.

Archimedean Property and Consequences

As a first serious application of completeness, we prove that N\mathbb{N}N is unbounded in R\mathbb{R}R — a statement that sounds obvious but genuinely requires completeness to prove (it fails, for instance, in certain non-Archimedean ordered fields that satisfy all the same field and order axioms).

Archimedean Property

For every x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N such that n>xn > xn>x. Equivalently, for every x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with x≤nx \le nx≤n.

Proof

Suppose, toward a contradiction, that some x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R satisfies x≥nx \ge nx≥n for all n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N — that is, xxx is an upper bound of N\mathbb{N}N. Then N\mathbb{N}N is nonempty and bounded above, so by completeness it has a supremum s=sup⁡Ns = \sup \mathbb{N}s=supN.

Since s−1<ss - 1 < ss−1<s, s−1s-1s−1 is not an upper bound of N\mathbb{N}N (by the Supremum Characterizations above), so there exists m∈Nm \in \mathbb{N}m∈N with s−1<m≤ss - 1 < m \le ss−1<m≤s. Adding 111 gives s<m+1s < m+1s<m+1. But m+1∈Nm + 1 \in \mathbb{N}m+1∈N, contradicting that sss is an upper bound of N\mathbb{N}N.

Hence no real xxx can bound N\mathbb{N}N above, so for every x∈Rx \in \mathbb{R}x∈R there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with n>xn > xn>x.

The Archimedean property is the engine behind several useful facts about how small 1/n1/n1/n can become, and about locating a real number among the integers. We collect these as corollaries.

Infimum of the Harmonic Sequence

If S={1/n:n∈N}S = \{1/n : n \in \mathbb{N}\}S={1/n:n∈N}, then inf⁡S=0.\inf S = 0.infS=0.

Proof

0 is a lower bound. For n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N, n>0n > 0n>0 implies 1/n>01/n > 01/n>0, so every element of SSS is positive; hence 0≤1/n0 \le 1/n0≤1/n for all nnn.

0 is the greatest lower bound. Let ε>0\varepsilon > 0ε>0. By the Archimedean property, there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N with n>1/εn > 1/\varepsilonn>1/ε, hence 1/n<ε1/n < \varepsilon1/n<ε. So ε\varepsilonε cannot be a lower bound of SSS. Since no positive number is a lower bound, 000 is the greatest lower bound.

Reciprocals Become Arbitrarily Small

For every t>0t > 0t>0, there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N such that 0<1n<t.0 < \frac{1}{n} < t.0<n1​<t.

This is the Archimedean property in disguise

This corollary is essentially a restatement of inf⁡{1/n}=0\inf\{1/n\} = 0inf{1/n}=0: since 000 is the greatest lower bound, no positive ttt can be a lower bound either, so some term of the sequence must dip below ttt.

Bounding a Real Number Between Consecutive Naturals

For every y∈Ry \in \mathbb{R}y∈R, there exists n∈Nn \in \mathbb{N}n∈N such that n−1≤y<n.n - 1 \le y < n.n−1≤y<n.

Proof

Let E={m∈N:y<m}E = \{m \in \mathbb{N} : y < m\}E={m∈N:y<m}. By the Archimedean property, EEE is nonempty. Let n=min⁡En = \min En=minE. Then y<ny < ny<n by definition of EEE, while n−1∉En - 1 \notin En−1∈/E (by minimality of nnn), so y≥n−1y \ge n-1y≥n−1. Therefore n−1≤y<nn - 1 \le y < nn−1≤y<n.
